P01: P01 cooks rice, soup, and meat-based dishes at a single stovetop station, with a yeast-based step and brief use of a food processor and oven during the session. They are stationary, cook from memory, and don't consult a phone or recipe. The defining characteristic of their cooking is that they constantly fight against material getting stuck to their tools — pieces of rice, onion, food, spice, soup, oil, or vinegar end up on spoons, spatulas, strainers, ladles, and lids, and a large share of their actions exists to dislodge those bits back into the appropriate container (the pot they came from, the bowl beneath, the bin, the plate). They reason about their cooking in concrete physical terms: where the stuck bits should go, where to make space for the next item, how to keep a wet lid from marking the counter, how to free a hand to open the fridge or wear a glove. Their reasoning is mostly mechanical and about-this-step rather than recipe-strategic, and cleanup as a parallel workflow is essentially absent during cooking.
P02: P02 is following a written recipe and consults the recipe sheet during the session to check the next step and recommended cooking times. They are baking (a raspberry-and-lemon curd, frangipane packets from a bag, cheese straws on a baking tray, quorn, pastry, items going into muffin trays and baking trays) while also doing parallel savory prep (peeling and cutting onions, cutting potatoes, garlic, tomatoes, mushrooms, bacon, fetching a squash bottle from the fridge). Their kitchen has a 'washing-up bowl' that sits at the sink and recurs throughout the session — the tap fills it, water from pans goes into it, hands are shaken into it. They reason about cooking in two-handed terms and frequently spell out which hand is holding what (right hand on the spatula while left hand holds the frying pan, freeing a specific hand to do the next thing). They go in and out of bags repeatedly to retrieve packets, and in and out of the fridge for ingredients. Cleanup is interleaved but moderate — the sponge is in regular use, but it does not dominate the session.
P03: P03 is making a complex savory meal — olive oil and garlic in the saucepan (with active concern that the garlic is burning when oil is too hot), spring greens and kale, aubergine that gets its sunflower oil squeezed out, mushrooms, lentils, potatoes cooked evenly, blueberries that need washing, raw eggs, a Spanish omelette, dumplings — alongside coffee-making (a V60 coffee filter, a coffee jug, ground coffee, beans). Their kitchen has a dishwasher that is part of the active cooking workflow rather than reserved for after the meal: the cheese grater, metal colander, and glass lids are placed directly into the top or bottom rack during the session, and they switch the washing-machine program on a dial. They reason about cooking with explicit awareness of ingredient state (oil temperature, whether greens are ready, whether potatoes are cooked evenly, whether coffee will brew evenly), and a large share of their actions exist to make room for the next thing they're already holding in the other hand. They protect their work surfaces — turning a spatula so the oily side faces up, placing lids so oil doesn't spill, turning saucepan handles out of the way. They are stationary, cook from memory.
P04: P04 is weighing and measuring ingredients on a kitchen scale, with bowls and tumblers repeatedly placed on the scale to weigh rice, fennel seeds, water, anise flour, and other items. They pour water between a tumbler and a measuring jug multiple times, and they are preparing dosa batter (shallow-frying chillies and lentils in a pan), scrambled eggs, and a sauce that gets fresh lemon juice squeezed into it. A pressure cooker is in use, and a ladle handles the batter. They are extremely fine-motion-focused — shaking, tipping, inverting, and rotating bowls and plates with controlled motions, often to expose a back side or to drain a thin layer of water. Cleanup is genuinely interleaved with cooking: they soap and wash items at the sink during the session, lather hands, wash handles under the tap, and use the dish drainer beside the sink to let washed items dry. Many of their actions are about getting excess water off a pan, plate, spoon, hand, or spatula, or about setting items aside specifically with the intent of washing them shortly afterwards. They cook from memory and rarely roam from their counter.
P05: P05 is making porridge, pesto (with a mortar and pestle), curry (with frozen spinach and coconut milk added with boiling water), pasta, and chops a banana — working from a single pan and a small set of spoons (regular, wooden, teaspoon, spatula). They cook from memory. Two strong, idiosyncratic habits define their reasoning. First, they care a great deal about not making the counter dirty or messy — placing spoons, chopping boards, and other tools down in specific ways so they don't transfer food or oil onto the counter. Second, when they free a hand or set something down, they reason about it in terms of the very next thing the freed hand is about to do (open the microwave, take an oven glove off, wash the knife, slide a chopped onion into the pan, fill the kettle, push mixture back into the mortar, pick up more things). They frequently tap, shake, or scrape a spoon or spatula against the pan to recover the specific food substance currently being cooked — and they think about that substance by name rather than generically. They also taste-test by licking spoons and are unembarrassed about saying so directly when they like a flavor.
P06: P06 is making pancakes and coffee in this session (this is a small observation sample of 13 questions, so confidence is limited). They reason about pancake-specific concerns (a smooth batter, regulating the gas-stove flame so the pancake doesn't burn), and they pour liquids between containers frequently — coffee from a jug into a cup placed on a tabletop, milk into a cup held in the left hand, frying-pan contents into a saucepan while still holding the spatula in the right hand. They mix spices together in a bowl by shaking and rub flour off their hands. They handle doors meaningfully, including moving between the kitchen and the living room during the session — they don't stay locked to one room. They operate the gas-stove knob, the water tap, the dustbin lid, and tilt plates into the dustbin while holding the lid open with the other hand. Cleanup shows up only as washing the frying pan and tongs at the sink.
P07: P07 is cooking a savory multi-component meal — chickpeas with olive oil and spices on a baking tray, mushrooms and rice in a frying pan, sweet potatoes tipped from a bowl onto the baking tray, butter scraped from a spatula into the frying pan, avocado tapped into a canister, onions sent into the frying pan, cheese grated into a bowl, a kettle of water. They cook from memory. Active cleanup is genuinely woven into the cooking rather than deferred to the end: they rinse the underside of the saucepan under the running tap, pour boiling water into the sink to clean it, wipe the kitchen island with a cloth, rinse soap off their hands, and turn the dishwasher on partway through the session. Many of their cooking actions exist to direct an ingredient to its destination — the last drop of olive oil onto the chickpeas, the rice and mushrooms back into the pan, the spices onto the chickpeas, the cheese into the bowl. They explain stir-actions in terms of keeping the frying pan moving so it doesn't burn, and they think about making room and space at the sink for the next pot or tray.
P08: P08 is running two cooking workflows in parallel, and both are equally central to their session. The first is nutrition tracking: a phone app is open throughout, and they record, update, log, or look up nutritional values and measurements of nearly every ingredient they handle by name — carrot, olive oil, balsamic glaze, coriander, cucumber, chicken, chicken thighs, chilli, green chili pepper, salad, couscous, spaghetti, spices, vegetables. They zero and adjust a kitchen scale before weighing, and they tap the phone screen, keyboard, or camera frequently between prep steps. The second is active dishwashing during cooking, with real soap. They clean knives, plates, cutting boards, spatulas, bowls, pans, the top of the pot, the colander, and a coffee mug at the sink, often using phrases like cleaning off soap or cleaning up the space, and they dry items afterwards with a tea towel — drying hands, fingers, the kitchen scale, the cutting board, the plate, the spatula, the top of the pot. Alongside both workflows they chop on a cutting board: pinching a carrot, onion, or cucumber steady with one hand and slicing with the knife in the right hand. All three threads — phone logging, soap-and-dry cleaning, and pinch-and-chop — are genuinely characteristic of P08's cooking, not occasional asides.
P09: P09 cooks by moving continuously between distinct kitchen stations and is the only cook in the cohort who regularly walks across the kitchen and across the room during a session. Their session centers on a potato-chicken-cheese dish that involves breadcrumbs and forms breaded balls — a 'potato chicken ball' that gets dipped in egg, placed alongside others on foil, and fried — alongside a curry, a hibiscus drink filtered through a slotted spoon, mango scooped from the skin, and a Spanish omelette. They cook from memory. Two habits define how they reason about their actions. First, when they pick something up, set something down, free a hand, or move across the room, the reason is almost always the very next concrete thing they're about to do, and they think about that next step in highly specific terms — picking up a particular sponge, scrubbing a particular knife, retrieving a particular serving spoon or potato masher, returning a particular bottle, dipping the potato-chicken ball in egg, stirring the onions, chopping the chicken, placing a specific bowl, turning a specific tap on or off. When in doubt about which next step is the real motivation, the named-concrete-object next step is more characteristic of them than a vague motive. Second, they clean continuously while cooking — a sponge is essentially always in play, washing-up liquid is fetched and used, knives and bowls and the pot are scrubbed mid-cook, the counter surface is wiped, the sink is the destination for many items. Despite this, when an action clearly serves a cooking step (stirring food, dipping into egg, cutting an ingredient), that cooking purpose is the real reason — they cook AND clean, they don't only clean.
